MINI MINUTES:

Hearing Re: PLAINTIFF SHASE HERNANDEZ's Motion For Attorney Fees.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Plaintiff's motion for attorney's fees is denied.

Plaintiff seeks post-settlement attorney's fees pursuant to Labor Code section 1102.5(j) for prevailing in this action. The award of such fees is discretionary. (Winston v. County of Los Angeles (2024) 107 Cal.App.5th 402, 408.) The court concludes that plaintiff is not entitled to any fees. The post-settlement dispute centered on how to characterize a portion of the settlement and whether defendant may file an IRS Form 1099. Both the court and mediator determined that there was no basis to preclude defendant from filing the form. As the court noted, "caselaw indicates that such a proscription should be expressly stated in the settlement. (See Ward v. American Family Life Assur. Co. of Columbus (D.S.C. 2006) 444 F.Supp.2d 540, 542-544; Duse v. International Business Machines Corp. (2d Cir. 2001) 252 F.3d 151, 158." (Order Granting Defendant San Francisco Conservatory of Music's Motion to Enforce Settlement, 2:13-15.) The court adopted defendant's position regarding enforcement of the judgment and the form of judgment. The requested fees were not reasonably incurred, there is no basis for their recovery, and the motion is denied.

Order signed.

Judge: Samuel K. Feng; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Not reported. (301/SKF)

Off Calendar Nov-14-2024 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT SAN FRANCISCO CONSERVATORY OF MUSIC'S Motion To Enforce Settlement Agreement As A Judgment [C.C.P. 664.6]

Defendant's motion to enforce the settlement agreement per CCP 664.6 is granted.

On May 20, 2024, the mediator presented a "Mediator's Proposal" to the parties to resolve this case. (Vartain Decl., Ex. A.) The Mediator's Proposal contemplated settlement of the case on the following terms: (1) defendant's payment of $485,000 to plaintiff; (2) a mutual release; (3) 10% of the settlement designated as backpay (after deduction for attorney's fees and costs); and (4) subsequent execution of a written settlement.

The parties subsequently became embroiled in a dispute regarding how to characterize the remaining 90% portion of the settlement and whether defendant may file an IRS Form 1099 with the IRS. To resolve this dispute, the parties entered into a joint stipulation for determination to the mediator. (Vartain Decl., B.) The stipulation was a writing signed by the parties and expressly noted that the Mediator's Proposal could be enforced per CCP 664.6. The parties therefore had an enforceable settlement.

Under the parties' settlement, there is no basis to preclude defendant from filing the Form 1099 if it chooses to do so. First, caselaw indicates that such a proscription should be expressly stated in the settlement. (See Ward v. American Family Life Assur. Co. of Columbus (D.S.C. 2006) 444 F.Supp.2d 540, 542-544; Duse v. International Business Machines Corp. (2d Cir. 2001) 252 F.3d 151, 158 ["The language of the Settlement Agreement in the present case is unambiguous. The Agreement did not by its terms forbid IBM to file a Form 1099."].) Second, the stipulation asked the mediator to determine the Form 1099 issue and he responded that defendant was not prohibited from filing the form. (Vartain Decl., Ex. F.) Defendant is therefore entitled to entry of judgment in conformance with the parties' settlement, which does not prevent the filing of the Form 1099.

Order is signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer; Clerk: W. Trupek; Not Reported (302/RBU)
